title: BUG: test_assignment_not_inplace incorrectly xfailed due to wrong assertion
body:
@jbrockmendel asked [claude] to open this.

## Summary

`tests/computation/test_eval.py::TestOperations::test_assignment_not_inplace` is marked `xfail` with reason `"Unknown: Omitted test_ in name prior."` The test was previously named without a `test_` prefix so pytest never collected it. When it was renamed, it started failing — but the failure is due to a bug **in the test itself**, not in pandas.

## The bug

The test checks that `df.eval("c = a + b", inplace=False)` does **not** modify `df` in place. However, the final assertion compares `df` (2 columns) against `expected` (3 columns, including `"c"`):

```python
actual = df.eval("c = a + b", inplace=False)
expected = df.copy()
expected["c"] = expected["a"] + expected["b"]
tm.assert_frame_equal(df, expected)  # wrong: compares `df` instead of `actual`
```

This assertion would only pass if `df` *was* modified in place — the exact bug the test was meant to guard against (GH#9297).

## Fix

- Remove the `xfail` marker
- Replace `tm.assert_frame_equal(df, expected)` with `tm.assert_frame_equal(actual, expected)`
- Add a check that `df` is unchanged, e.g. `assert list(df.columns) == ["a", "b"]`

title: BUG: unique().item() query no longer possible since unique() method no longer returns array
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
pd.__version__
# '3.0.0'
df = pd.DataFrame([
  {"person": "John", "userName": "Johnny1234"}, 
  {"person": "Jane", "userName": "Jane3"}
])
person = df.query("userName == 'Johnny1234'")['person'].unique().item()

# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
# AttributeError: 'ArrowStringArray' object has no attribute 'item
```

### Issue Description

`unique` series method now returns array type rather than a series of unique items, breaking the use of .unique().item(), see discussions https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/18262#issuecomment-546746982 and https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/29250

Previously the use of the method would allow one to query a column with the expectation that only one result was returned. If none or more were returned, .item() would return a value error. 

The workaround for now is to do rewrap the array in a series object

```
person = pd.Series(df.query("userName == 'Johnny1234'")['person'].unique()).item()
```

### Expected Behavior

We would expect the following response
```
# 'John'
```

### Installed Versions

<details>

<summary>Installed Versions</summary>

```
INSTALLED VERSIONS
------------------
commit                : 366ccdfcd8ed1e5543bfb6d4ee0c9bc519898670

python-bits           : 64
```

title: BUG: pandas 3.0.0 and pyarrow 21.0.0, pd.merge left join with 3 keys incorrectly matches a NaN key with a non-NaN value.
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

print(f"pandas version: {pd.__version__}")

df_left = pd.DataFrame({
    'key1': ['XBSP'],
    'key2': ['TARGET'],
    'key3': ['BROKER_A'],
})

df_right = pd.DataFrame({
    'rkey1': pd.array(['XBSP', 'XBSP', 'XBMF', 'XBMF', 'XBSP', np.nan], dtype='str'),
    'key2':  pd.array(['TARGET', 'F0', 'F0', 'F1', 'F1', 'TARGET'], dtype='str'),
    'key3':  pd.array(['BROKER_A', 'B0', 'B0', 'B1', 'B1', 'BROKER_A'], dtype='str'),
})

result = pd.merge(df_left, df_right, how='left',
                  left_on=['key1', 'key2', 'key3'],
                  right_on=['rkey1', 'key2', 'key3'])

print(f"\nExpected: 1 row")
print(f"Got:      {len(result)} rows")
print(result)
```

### Issue Description

pd.merge left join with 3 keys incorrectly matches a NaN key with a non-NaN value.

**Condition:** This issue reproduces with `pyarrow==21.0.0`.

### Expected Behavior

1 row (only the exact match XBSP/TARGET/BROKER_A)

### Installed Versions


INSTALLED VERSIONS
------------------

python-bits           : 64

title: BUG: pandas read_sas not working anymore
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```
python
import pandas as pd

file_path="abfss://x@x.dfs.core.windows.net/input/output_be.sas7bdat"
print(mssparkutils.fs.ls(file_path))
pandas_df = pd.read_sas(file_path)
print(pandas_df)
```

### Issue Description

We have this piece of code in our Synapse Python notebook and suddenly it stopped working as expected. Error: "**_ValueError: Length of values (72851) does not match length of index (57013)_**"

```
----> 5 pandas_df = pd.read_sas(file_path, format='sas7bdat')
      6 print(pandas_df)

File ~/cluster-env/clonedenv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pandas/util/_decorators.py:331, in deprecate_nonkeyword_arguments.<locals>.decorate.<locals>.wrapper(*args, **kwargs)
    325 if len(args) > num_allow_args:
    326     warnings.warn(
    327         msg.format(arguments=_format_argument_list(allow_args)),
    328         FutureWarning,
    329         stacklevel=find_stack_level(),
    330     )
--> 331 return func(*args, **kwargs)

File ~/cluster-env/clonedenv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pandas/io/sas/sasreader.py:175, in read_sas(filepath_or_buffer, format, index, encoding, chunksize, iterator, compression)
    172     return reader
    174 with reader:
--> 175     return reader.read()

File ~/cluster-env/clonedenv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pandas/io/sas/sas7bdat.py:742, in SAS7BDATReader.read(self, nrows)
    739 p = Parser(self)
    740 p.read(nrows)
--> 742 rslt = self._chunk_to_dataframe()
    743 if self.index is not None:
    744     rslt = rslt.set_index(self.index)

File ~/cluster-env/clonedenv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pandas/io/sas/sas7bdat.py:795, in SAS7BDATReader._chunk_to_dataframe(self)
    793     jb += 1
    794 elif self._column_types[j] == b"s":
--> 795     rslt[name] = pd.Series(self._string_chunk[js, :], index=ix)
    796     if self.convert_text and (self.encoding is not None):
    797         rslt[name] = self._decode_string(rslt[name].str)

File ~/cluster-env/clonedenv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pandas/core/series.py:461, in Series.__init__(self, data, index, dtype, name, copy, fastpath)
    459     index = default_index(len(data))
    460 elif is_list_like(data):
--> 461     com.require_length_match(data, index)
    463 # create/copy the manager
    464 if isinstance(data, (SingleBlockManager, SingleArrayManager)):

File ~/cluster-env/clonedenv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pandas/core/common.py:571, in require_length_match(data, index)
    567 """
    568 Check the length of data matches the length of the index.
    569 """
    570 if len(data) != len(index):
--> 571     raise ValueError(
    572         "Length of values "
    573         f"({len(data)}) "
    574         "does not match length of index "
    575         f"({len(index)})"
    576     )
```

### Expected Behavior

It should return the Pandas df

title: BUG: Reindexing with fill_value
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
df = pd.DataFrame(data=["HELLO"], index=range(1, 10), columns=["c"], dtype="string")
df.reindex(columns=["a", "b", "c"], fill_value="test")
```

### Issue Description

Reindexing with `fill_value` argument does not work anymore since Pandas 3.0.0
It generates an inappropriate `AssertionError`

### Expected Behavior

It should not raise an assertion error and just add two columns  `a` and `b` with value `test`

### Installed Versions

<details>
>>> pd.show_versions()

INSTALLED VERSIONS
------------------
commit                : 366ccdfcd8ed1e5543bfb6d4ee0c9bc519898670
python                : 3.14.0
python-bits           : 64
OS                    : Darwin
OS-release            : 25.2.0
Version               : Darwin Kernel Version 25.2.0: Tue Nov 18 21:08:48 PST 2025; root:xnu-12377.61.12~1/RELEASE_ARM64_T8132
machine               : arm64

title: BUG: pd.to_timedelta unit inconsistently handled
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
print(pd.__version__)
print(pd.to_timedelta([0.5, 2], unit="m"))
print(pd.to_timedelta([0.5], unit="m"))
print(pd.to_timedelta([2], unit="m"))
```

### Issue Description

In the latest version, the unit argument of `pd.to_timedelta` seems to be inconsistently handled.
When I pass 0.5 and 2 with unit ='m'  together I get 30 seconds and 120 nanos.
When I pass 0.5 alone with unit = 'm'  I get 30 seconds.
When I pass 2 alone with unit ='m' I get 2 minutes.

### Expected Behavior

Using version 2.3.3 of `pandas`, get the expected result:
```
TimedeltaIndex(['0 days 00:00:30', '0 days 00:02:00'], dtype='timedelta64[ns]', freq=None)
TimedeltaIndex(['0 days 00:00:30'], dtype='timedelta64[ns]', freq=None)
TimedeltaIndex(['0 days 00:02:00'], dtype='timedelta64[ns]', freq=None)
```


### Installed Versions

title: BUG: `pd.read_hdf` unable to retrieve a pd.Series with dtype as "datetime64" since v3.0.0
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [ ] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

pd.read_hdf(Path("path_to_old_store"), key="datetime_data_exported_via_to_hdf_in_earlier_pandas_version")
```

### Issue Description

From pandas 3.0.0 an error occurs when retrieving via `pd.read_hdf` a `pd.Series` with dtype as "datetime64", i.e. older data that was saved via `pd.to_hdf` before (I imagine) pandas started writing datetime64 units.

It seems that the equivalent situation for "timedelta64" is accommodated for by [this if clause](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/8ff98463033bd413dabdb5036a92744de2d31fe4/pandas/io/pytables.py#L3095) which sets the default unit to 'ns' in the event that the dtype is merely "timedelta64". However, for "datetime64" the dtype is passed through to `_set_tz` which in turn gets the unit [here](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/8ff98463033bd413dabdb5036a92744de2d31fe4/pandas/io/pytables.py#L5108) from `np.datetime_data` which returns the unit of a "datetime64" dtype as 'generic'. This 'generic' value ends up being passed to the 'unit' parameter of `DatetimeTZDtype` and that function throws an error as a `TimeUnit` is expected, i.e. one of "s", "ms", "us", "ns".

I'm not sure if this is a bug or rather a matter of `pd.read_hdf` in Pandas 3.0.0 not being backwards compatible with data exported from earlier versions? The fact that the equivalent situation for 'timedelta64' appears to have been accommodated seems to suggest it's a bug...?

Might the fix be as simple as including the following to immediately follow [pytables.py L3090](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/8ff98463033bd413dabdb5036a92744de2d31fe4/pandas/io/pytables.py#L3089):
```python
if dtype == "datetime64":
	dtype = "datetime64[ns]"
```
?

Cheers
Marcus

### Expected Behavior

For data imported via `pd.read_hdf` would have expected pandas 3.0.0 to infer datetime resolution where not otherwise defined.

### Installed Versions

INSTALLED VERSIONS
------------------

title: BUG: `DataFrame.reindex` with multiple `columns` and a string `fill_value` raises `AssertionError` with `3.0.0`
body:
### Pandas version checks

- [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd

pd.DataFrame(data={"a": [0]}).reindex(columns=["a", "b", "c"], fill_value="missing")
```

### Issue Description

Running `pd.DataFrame.index` with multiple `columns` and a string `fill_value`  raises `AssertionError` with `3.0.0`:
```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../example.py", line 7, in <module>
    pd.DataFrame({"a": [0]}).reindex(columns=["a", "b", "c"], fill_value="missing")
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/frame.py", line 6078, in reindex
    return super().reindex(
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/generic.py", line 5476, in reindex
    return self._reindex_axes(
           ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/generic.py", line 5503, in _reindex_axes
    obj = obj._reindex_with_indexers(
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/generic.py", line 5547, in _reindex_with_indexers
    new_data = new_data.reindex_indexer(
               ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/internals/managers.py", line 839, in reindex_indexer
    new_blocks = list(
                 ^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/internals/managers.py", line 959, in _slice_take_blocks_ax0
    yield self._make_na_block(
          ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/internals/managers.py", line 1024, in _make_na_block
    block_values = make_na_array(dtype, shape, fill_value)
                   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File ".../lib/python3.12/site-packages/pandas/core/internals/managers.py", line 2521, in make_na_array
    assert ncols == 1, ncols
           ^^^^^^^^^^
AssertionError: 2
````

This does not happen with `2.3.3`.

### Expected Behavior

The example outputs the following `DataFrame`
```python
   a        b        c
0  0  missing  missing
```

### Installed Versions

python-bits           : 64
